relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Nathan enters the palace with a story capable of passing the defenses of a powerful man.
The rich man possesses abundance beyond need.
The poor household loves one vulnerable creature as family, sharing food, cup, and embrace.
The rich man protects his own abundance by taking the poor family's beloved lamb.
David instantly recognizes predation when it is disguised as another man's story.
He demands multiplied restoration because the taker showed no compassion.
Nathan removes the disguise: the king himself is the rich man who took.
The oracle contrasts David's abundance with his decision to seize what royal power placed within reach.
Uriah's murder and Bathsheba's taking are named together as one abuse of throne, sex, and sword.
Violence planted through command returns as violence within the royal household.
The announced consequence again places women at risk, revealing a patriarchal theology that must be resisted rather than imitated.
Public exposure answers secret abuse, but survivors cannot ethically be turned into instruments of that exposure.
David confesses in one sentence and is told he will live; survival is not the same as completed repair.
The consequence is transferred to a child who did not cause the abuse, an unbearable claim that cannot become pastoral explanation.
Nathan leaves, and the child born within the crisis becomes gravely ill.
David fasts and lies on the ground, seeking life for the child.
Household elders attempt to lift and feed him, but grief closes him off.
When the child dies, servants fear the bereaved king may harm himself.
Whispered fear tells David what no one wants to say aloud.
He rises, tends his body, worships, returns home, and eats.
His servants ask how grief changed so abruptly after death.
David explains that while life remained, he hoped mercy might alter the outcome.
After death he names irreversible separation rather than pretending ritual can undo it.
Bathsheba later bears Solomon, yet later life does not replace the child who died or erase the violence that preceded him.
Nathan gives the child another name, Jedidiah—beloved of YHWH.
The narrative returns immediately to Joab's conquest of Rabbah.
Joab reports control of the city's water and royal center.
He invites David to claim the public credit for final capture.
The king mobilizes the remaining force and takes the city.
Ammonite royal wealth and crown are transferred to David through conquest.
The inhabitants are subjected to brutal forced labor or torture, keeping empire's cost at the chapter's end.

contemplative_note: 'You are the man' is the sentence that breaks royal self-exemption. Yet the chapter also shows how even a true confrontation can leave survivors, children, women, soldiers, and conquered people carrying consequences. Spiritual accountability must move beyond private confession toward safety, truth, repair, and transformed power.
